steps.title: Test claims with evidence
steps.body: Hypothesis testing asks whether sample data give enough evidence to reject a default claim. This chapter covers null and alternative hypotheses, test statistics, p-values, significance levels, and the two kinds of errors you can make.

steps.title: State the null hypothesis
steps.context: A factory claims its bolts have a mean length of 50 mm. A quality inspector suspects the mean has drifted.
steps.prompt: What is the correct null hypothesis H0?
hints: The null hypothesis states the status quo or claimed value.
Hypotheses are statements about the population parameter mu, not the sample mean.

feedback.wrong: The null is the claimed equality about the population parameter.

solution.body: H0: mu = 50. The null always expresses 'no change' or the claimed value, using the parameter mu.
solution.takeaway: Null hypotheses are statements of equality about a population parameter.

steps.title: Choose the alternative
steps.context: The inspector only cares whether the mean has changed in either direction.
steps.prompt: Which alternative hypothesis Ha matches a two-sided test?
hints: Two-sided means you care about deviations in both directions.
The ≠ symbol captures 'different in either direction'.

feedback.wrong: A two-sided alternative uses the not-equal sign.

solution.body: Ha: mu ≠ 50 is two-sided because a drift in either direction matters.
solution.takeaway: Match the alternative's direction to the research question.

steps.title: Compute the z test statistic
steps.context: Sample of n = 64 bolts gives x-bar = 50.5. Assume sigma = 2.
hints: Standard error is sigma/sqrt(n) = 2/8 = 0.25.

feedback.wrong: Subtract the null value, then divide by the standard error.

solution.takeaway: A test statistic measures how many standard errors the estimate sits from the null value.

steps.title: Interpret the p-value
steps.context: A test produces a two-sided p-value of 0.046 at significance level alpha = 0.05.

options.label: Accept H0 because p is small
Fail to reject H0 because p < alpha

hints: Compare the p-value to alpha.
If p < alpha, the evidence is strong enough to reject H0.

feedback.wrong: Reject H0 when the p-value is below the significance level.

solution.body: Since 0.046 < 0.05, reject H0. We never 'accept' H0 — we only fail to reject it.
solution.takeaway: Decision rule: reject H0 when p-value < alpha.

steps.title: Define a p-value
steps.context: Students often misread what a p-value represents.
steps.prompt: Which statement correctly describes a p-value?

options.label: The probability that H0 is true
The probability of data at least this extreme, assuming H0 is true
The probability of making a Type I error in this study

hints: A p-value is computed assuming the null hypothesis holds.
It measures how surprising the observed data are under H0.

feedback.wrong: A p-value is a conditional probability of extreme data given H0.

solution.body: A p-value is P(data at least this extreme | H0 true). It is not the probability that H0 is true.
solution.takeaway: P-values describe data given the null, not the probability the null is correct.

steps.title: Type I vs Type II error
steps.context: Suppose the bolts are actually fine (H0 is true) but the test rejects H0.
steps.prompt: What kind of error is this?
hints: Type I = rejecting a true null.
Type II = failing to reject a false null.

feedback.wrong: Rejecting a true null is a Type I error.

solution.body: Rejecting a true H0 is a Type I error, whose probability equals alpha.
solution.takeaway: Type I = false alarm; Type II = missed detection.

steps.title: Significance and Type I rate
steps.context: A researcher runs many tests at alpha = 0.01 when H0 is true every time.
steps.prompt: In the long run, what fraction of these tests will reject H0 (as a decimal)?
hints: Alpha is the long-run Type I error rate.
If H0 is always true, rejections happen at rate alpha.

feedback.wrong: The Type I error rate equals alpha.

solution.body: About 0.01 (1%) of tests reject H0, because alpha is the Type I error rate.
solution.takeaway: Choosing alpha sets your tolerated false-positive rate.

steps.title: Capstone — set up the test
steps.context: A coffee chain claims mean service time is 180 seconds. A sample of n = 100 visits gives x-bar = 174 with known sigma = 30. Managers want to know if service is faster.
steps.prompt: What are the hypotheses for 'service is faster'?
hints: 'Faster' means a smaller mean time.
The alternative carries the direction of the claim.

feedback.wrong: A one-sided 'faster' claim uses mu < 180 as the alternative.

solution.body: H0: mu = 180 vs Ha: mu < 180, a left-tailed test.
solution.takeaway: Capstone step 1: translate the research claim into directional hypotheses.

feedback.wrong: Use (x-bar − mu0)/SE; the result should be negative here.

solution.takeaway: Capstone step 2: standardize the estimate into a test statistic.

steps.title: Capstone — conclusion
steps.context: The left-tailed p-value for z = −2 is about 0.023, with alpha = 0.05.

options.label: Reject H0: evidence that service is faster than 180s
Fail to reject H0: no evidence of any difference
Accept H0: service time equals 180s exactly

hints: Compare 0.023 to alpha = 0.05.
State the conclusion in the context of the claim.

feedback.wrong: Since p < alpha, reject H0 and report evidence of faster service.

solution.body: Because 0.023 < 0.05, reject H0; there is significant evidence that mean service time is below 180 seconds.
solution.takeaway: Capstone flow: hypotheses, test statistic, p-value, then a context-rich decision.

steps.title: Chapter 7 complete
steps.body:
You can now frame null and alternative hypotheses, compute z test statistics, interpret p-values correctly, and weigh Type I against Type II errors.

Next you'll extend these ideas to comparing two groups.
